result:
I keep coming back to the idea that every object is a slow-motion event.  
The coffee mug on my desk is not a *thing*; it is a quarrel between silica, alumina, and heat that lost its urgency 1,200 °C ago. The ring on my finger is a renegotiated asteroid—gold atoms that once screamed past each other in the vacuum, now politely holding hands because the earth’s gravity bullied them into a lattice. Even the words you are reading are a parade of extinct forests: the “e” started as a reed on some Mesopotamian riverbank, became papyrus, parchment, a pixel, and will end as phosphor decay on a screen you eventually shut off. Nothing settles; it only cools enough to pretend.

We are told to “live in the present,” but the present is the one place material physics forbids us to inhabit. Light needs time to bounce off the face you love and reach your retina; neurons need milliseconds to bicker about what it means. By the time you notice the world, the world has already moved on. We are always browsing yesterday’s edition of the universe, never the fresh print. That means every kiss, every funeral, every sunrise is nostalgia in real time. I find that weirdly comforting—like discovering the train you’re worrying about has already left, so you might as well sit on the bench and eat your orange.

Sometimes I think the only honest art form is rust. It doesn’t argue, doesn’t brand, doesn’t try to go viral. It just keeps telling the truth: that metastability is as close to forever as we get. A fence post forgetting it was ever galvanized; a bicycle surrendering its sky-blue paint back to the iron underneath. We photograph it, call it “beautiful decay,” and swipe right, but rust isn’t performing for us. It’s simply reconciling two incompatible timelines: the human one that needed a fence, and the geological one that never asked for fences in the first place.

I write these fragments on the edge of sleep, when the house sounds shift from human to tectonic: the fridge sighs like an old dog, the wooden beams remember they were once vertical in a forest, and the pipes rehearse arias about pressure fatigue. If I listen long enough, language unthreads. Words stop being signifiers and become objects again—little ceramic tiles clacking in a bag, waiting for a mosaic I’ll never finish. That’s when I suspect that thoughts, too, are just slower weather: inner hailstones of unsorted emotion falling through the warm front of grammar. They melt, re-form, fall again, and one day the atmosphere changes just enough so the pattern can’t reconstruct itself. We call that forgetting; the sky calls it Tuesday.

So I keep the mug, the ring, the sentences, not to possess them but to witness their long defeat. We are curators of temporary constellations: marriages, skin, civilizations, even the laws of physics may be seasonal. The kindest thing we can do is notice the flicker, the way you notice a stranger’s unmatched socks and smile without explanation. The flicker is the part that refuses to be inventory, the part still busy becoming. Hold it too tight and it cools into fact; ignore it and it drifts off into rumor. The trick—no, the craft—is to cup it like water in two open palms: enough pressure to keep it from spilling, not so much that it leaks through your lifeline.

Tomorrow the kettle will boil again. Molecules will race each other through stainless-steel corridors, the steam will write short-lived calligraphy on the window, and I will pretend I am making coffee rather than rehearsing the dissolution of mountains. That’s fine. The pretending is part of the event.
